datasets_used: 70,000 compositions from Ti-Fe-Al-V-Ni-Nb-Zr-Mn-Co design space with phase fractions at 7 temperatures (CALPHAD)
hyperparameters: Oversampling strategy for class imbalance; 41-dimensional feature space; 6 MDN models (FCC/BCC/Laves/Sigma/Heusler/Liquid)
headline_result: R² of 0.99 for Laves phase prediction
ablations: Feature reduction from 41 to 12 most important features preserved accuracy; further reduction decreased accuracy and increased epistemic uncertainty
stated_limitations: Existing machine learning approaches rely on deterministic mappings from composition-derived features to phase labels, neglecting the uncertainty inherent in such predictions
The paper addresses this by using Mixture Density Networks (MDNs) to predict phase fractions and quantify aleatoric uncertainty
unstated_weaknesses: High domain specificity to refractory multi-principal element alloys with unclear transferability to medical imaging
No mention of dataset size, quality, or potential biases in the data
Computational requirements and scalability of the approach not specified
Limited discussion of model interpretability and explainability
threats_to_validity: Domain specificity threatens generalization to medical imaging applications
Performance at extreme temperatures not explicitly evaluated
Active learning for out-of-domain discovery may not translate well to medical imaging
Uncertainty quantification method may not be optimal for all types of uncertainty
open_questions: How well does the MDN approach generalize to other domains like medical imaging?
What is the computational cost of training and deploying these models?
How does the active learning component perform with different types of uncertainty?
What is the impact of the size and quality of the training data on model performance?
future_work: Applying the deep learning framework for phase fraction prediction and uncertainty quantification to other domains
Using active learning for out-of-domain discovery in new applications
gaps_this_enables: Demonstrates the value of uncertainty-aware deep learning in materials science
Provides a framework for active learning that could be adapted to medical imaging
Opens possibilities for calibration-aware approaches in other domains
relevance_to_topic: This paper demonstrates a calibration-aware approach using active learning and uncertainty quantification that could be adapted for medical image classification.
reusable_assets: Mixture Density Network (MDN) framework for uncertainty-aware predictions
Active learning approach for out-of-domain discovery
best_result: R² of 0.99 for Laves phase prediction
limitations_stated: Existing machine learning approaches rely on deterministic mappings from composition-derived features to phase labels, neglecting the uncertainty inherent in such predictions
The paper addresses this by using Mixture Density Networks (MDNs) to predict phase fractions and quantify aleatoric uncertainty
title: Uncertainty-aware phase fraction prediction and active-learning-guided out-of-domain discovery of refractory multi-principal element alloys
